sections.heading: Threshold for filing application
sections.body_text: This section specifies the minimum amount of default required to initiate the corporate insolvency resolution process (CIRP). As per the Act, the default threshold is Rs. 1 crore, subject to amendments by the government.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Persons who may initiate CIRP
sections.body_text: This section allows a financial creditor, operational creditor, or the corporate debtor itself to initiate the corporate insolvency resolution process against a defaulting corporate debtor.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Time limit for completion of CIRP
sections.body_text: The corporate insolvency resolution process must be completed within 180 days from the date of admission of the application, with a one-time extension of up to 90 days if approved by the Adjudicating Authority.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Moratorium
sections.body_text: Upon the commencement of CIRP, a moratorium is declared, prohibiting the institution of suits, continuation of pending proceedings, and enforcement of security interests against the corporate debtor.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Public announcement of CIRP
sections.body_text: The resolution professional must make a public announcement of the initiation of CIRP, inviting claims from creditors and providing details of the process.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Committee of creditors
sections.body_text: This section establishes the committee of creditors, comprising financial creditors of the corporate debtor, which is responsible for making key decisions during the CIRP.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Appointment of resolution professional
sections.body_text: The committee of creditors has the authority to appoint or replace the resolution professional by a majority vote of not less than 66%.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Role of resolution professional
sections.body_text: The resolution professional manages the operations of the corporate debtor during the CIRP and ensures compliance with the provisions of the Code.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Meetings of committee of creditors
sections.body_text: This section outlines the procedure for conducting meetings of the committee of creditors, including quorum requirements and voting thresholds.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Submission of resolution plan
sections.body_text: Resolution applicants may submit resolution plans to the resolution professional, which must comply with the requirements specified under the Code.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Approval of resolution plan
sections.body_text: The resolution plan, once approved by the committee of creditors, must be submitted to the Adjudicating Authority for final approval and implementation.
sections.chapter: Part II - Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process

sections.heading: Initiation of liquidation
sections.body_text: If the resolution plan is not approved within the prescribed timeline or is rejected, the corporate debtor enters liquidation as per the provisions of this section.
sections.chapter: Part II - Liquidation Process

sections.heading: Preferential transactions
sections.body_text: This section deals with transactions that unfairly prefer one creditor over others and allows the resolution professional to apply for their avoidance.
sections.chapter: Part II - Avoidance Transactions

sections.heading: Order in case of preferential transactions
sections.body_text: The Adjudicating Authority may pass orders to reverse or compensate for preferential transactions that are detrimental to the interests of creditors.
sections.chapter: Part II - Avoidance Transactions

sections.heading: Undervalued transactions
sections.body_text: This section addresses transactions where assets are transferred for significantly less than their value, which may be avoided to protect creditors' interests.
sections.chapter: Part II - Avoidance Transactions

sections.heading: Extortionate credit transactions
sections.body_text: This section allows the resolution professional to challenge credit transactions that impose exorbitant terms on the corporate debtor.
sections.chapter: Part II - Avoidance Transactions

sections.heading: Dissolution of corporate debtor
sections.body_text: Upon completion of the liquidation process, the corporate debtor is dissolved, and the Adjudicating Authority passes an order to that effect.
sections.chapter: Part II - Liquidation Process

sections.heading: Jurisdiction of NCLT
sections.body_text: The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) is designated as the Adjudicating Authority for corporate persons under the Code, with jurisdiction over insolvency and liquidation proceedings.
sections.chapter: Part II - Adjudicating Authority

sections.heading: Agreements with foreign countries
sections.body_text: This section empowers the Central Government to enter into bilateral agreements with foreign countries for enforcing provisions related to cross-border insolvency.
sections.chapter: Part V - Cross-Border Insolvency

sections.heading: Letter of request to a foreign court
sections.body_text: The Adjudicating Authority may issue a letter of request to a foreign court or authority for assistance in dealing with assets or evidence located in that country.
sections.chapter: Part V - Cross-Border Insolvency
